title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: table summarizes recommended measures variable type. PRE = Proportional Reduction Error. measures quantify much knowing one variable reduces prediction error . functions accept contingency table (class table, typically xtabs() table()).

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: assoc_measures() computes available measures : useful exploratory analysis. reporting, pick measure matches variable types.

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Cramer’s V measures strength association two nominal variables. ranges 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value. p-value tests null hypothesis association using Pearson chi-squared test.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) cramer_v(tbl) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 1.356677e-01 7.909264e-02 1.913716e-01 2.012877e-05

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: 2x2 tables, Phi equivalent Cramer’s V. Unlike V, Phi can negative table 2x2, indicating direction association.
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) phi(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #> 0.005910601 0.000000000 0.063077970 0.839443630

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: contingency coefficient alternative Cramer’s V. upper bound depends table dimensions, makes harder compare across tables different sizes.
code: contingency_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 1.344361e-01           NA           NA 2.012877e-05

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: variables ordinal (ordered factors), measures account ordering appropriate Cramer’s V.

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Gamma ranges -1 +1. ignores tied pairs, makes sensitive direction association tends overestimate strength many ties. positive value means higher values one variable tend occur higher values . p-value tests H0: Gamma = 0 using Wald z-test.
code: tbl_ord <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) gamma_gk(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 3.104791e-01 2.376258e-01 3.833324e-01 6.667393e-17

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Tau-b adjusts ties ranges -1 +1. generally preferred Gamma square near-square tables.
code: kendall_tau_b(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 2.045524e-01 1.552195e-01 2.538854e-01 4.410418e-16

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Tau-c similar Tau-b adjusts rectangular tables number rows columns differ.
code: kendall_tau_c(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 1.996409e-01 1.509952e-01 2.482866e-01 8.720015e-16

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Somers’ D asymmetric measure: distinguishes dependent independent variable. default, row variable treated dependent (D(R|C)).
code: somers_d(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 2.076131e-01 1.573452e-01 2.578810e-01 5.730469e-16

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: measures answer specific question: much knowing column variable reduce error predicting row variable (vice versa)?

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Lambda measures proportional reduction classification error. can equal zero even variables associated, modal category change across columns.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ employment_status + education, data = sochealth) lambda_gk(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> estimate ci_lower ci_upper  p_value  #>        0        0        0       NA

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Tau measures proportional reduction error predicting row variable column variable, using full distribution (just mode).
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(tbl, detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #> 0.011754531 0.002910365 0.020598697 0.009189229

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: uncertainty coefficient (Theil’s U) based entropy. measures much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty .
code: uncertainty_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #> 0.014340586 0.004867184 0.023813987 0.003007778

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: Yule’s Q defined 2x2 tables . ranges -1 +1 equivalent Gamma 2x2 tables.
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) yule_q(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #>  0.01450794 -0.12582192  0.15483780  0.83942419

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: cross_tab() can automatically select appropriate measure via assoc_measure = "auto" (default). variables ordered factors, picks Kendall’s Tau-b; otherwise uses Cramer’s V. can override automatic choice:

title: Association measures for contingency tables
text: functions support confidence intervals via detail = TRUE. confidence level defaults 95% can changed conf_level: get estimate p-value (CI), pass conf_level = NULL:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = 0.99) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 1.269149e-01 5.310210e-02 1.993484e-01 8.352614e-07 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #>     estimate      p_value  #> 1.269149e-01 8.352614e-07

title: Getting started with spicy
text: varlist() (shortcut vl()) gives compact overview every variable data frame: name, label, representative values, class, number distinct values, valid observations, missing values. can also select specific columns tidyselect syntax:
code: varlist(sochealth) #> Non-interactive session: use `tbl = TRUE` to return the table. varlist(sochealth, starts_with("bmi"), income, weight) #> Non-interactive session: use `tbl = TRUE` to return the table.

title: Getting started with spicy
text: freq() produces frequency tables counts, percentages, (optionally) valid cumulative percentages. Weighted frequencies use weights argument. rescale = TRUE, total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: freq(sochealth, education) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category │ Values           Freq.  Percent  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Lower secondary    261     21.8  #>           │ Upper secondary    539     44.9  #>           │ Tertiary           400     33.3  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │                   1200    100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth freq(sochealth, education, weights = weight, rescale = TRUE) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category │ Values            Freq.  Percent  #> ──────────┼────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Lower secondary  259.49     21.6  #>           │ Upper secondary  548.55     45.7  #>           │ Tertiary         391.95     32.7  #> ──────────┼────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │                    1200    100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth #> Weight: weight (rescaled)

title: Getting started with spicy
text: table_apa() builds publication-ready cross-tabulation report crossing one grouping variable one many row variables. supports multiple output formats. output = "gt" get formatted table suitable R Markdown Quarto documents: Use assoc_ci = TRUE display confidence interval inline: formats include "tinytable", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard", "word". "wide" "long" formats return data frames processing:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity", "dentist_12m"),   group_var = "education",   output = "gt" ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "smoking",   group_var = "education",   output = "gt",   assoc_ci = TRUE ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   output = "wide" ) #>            Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1           smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2           smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #> 3 physical_activity    No               177              67.8               310 #> 4 physical_activity   Yes                84              32.2               229 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #> 3              57.5        163       40.8     650    54.2 8.333584e-12 #> 4              42.5        237       59.2     550    45.8 8.333584e-12 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677 #> 3  0.2061986 #> 4  0.2061986

title: Getting started with spicy
text: mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n() compute row-wise statistics across selected columns, automatic handling missing values.
code: d <- sochealth[, c("bmi", "wellbeing_score", "political_position")] d$row_mean <- mean_n(sochealth, c("bmi", "wellbeing_score", "political_position")) d$n_missing <- count_n(sochealth, c("bmi", "wellbeing_score", "political_position"), special = "NA") head(d) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>     bmi wellbeing_score political_position row_mean n_missing #>   <dbl>           <dbl>              <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1  27.7            90.9                  7     41.9         0 #> 2  22.6            79.5                  4     35.4         0 #> 3  23.2            44.2                  6     24.5         0 #> 4  26.7            62.4                 10     33.0         0 #> 5  19.7            95.8                  4     39.8         0 #> 6  26.4            76                    6     36.1         0

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: minimum, provide data frame, one row variables, grouping variable: default output "wide" style = "raw", returns data frame numeric values suitable processing.
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity", "dentist_12m"),   group_var = "education" ) #>            Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1           smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2           smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #> 3 physical_activity    No               177              67.8               310 #> 4 physical_activity   Yes                84              32.2               229 #> 5       dentist_12m    No               113              43.3               174 #> 6       dentist_12m   Yes               148              56.7               365 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #> 3              57.5        163       40.8     650    54.2 8.333584e-12 #> 4              42.5        237       59.2     550    45.8 8.333584e-12 #> 5              32.3         67       16.8     354    29.5 3.883413e-13 #> 6              67.7        333       83.2     846    70.5 3.883413e-13 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677 #> 3  0.2061986 #> 4  0.2061986 #> 5  0.2182388 #> 6  0.2182388

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: table_apa() supports several output formats. table summarizes options:

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: "gt" format produces table APA-style borders, column spanners, proper alignment:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity", "dentist_12m"),   group_var = "education",   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   output = "tinytable" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: Use style = "report" "wide" "long" get formatted character columns (ready display), style = "raw" numeric values (ready analysis):
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "smoking",   group_var = "education",   output = "wide",   style = "report" ) #>   Variable Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking                                                       #> 2       No               179              69.6               415 #> 3      Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %      p Cramer's V #> 1                                                         < .001        .14 #> 2              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8                   #> 3              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: default, table_apa() uses variable names row headers. Use labels argument provide human-readable labels:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: default, table_apa() reports Cramer’s V nominal variables automatically switches Kendall’s Tau-b variables ordered factors. Override assoc_measure: Add confidence intervals assoc_ci = TRUE. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable), CI shown inline: data formats (wide, long, excel, clipboard), separate CI lower CI upper columns added:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "smoking",   group_var = "education",   assoc_measure = "phi",   output = "gt" ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "gt" ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "smoking",   group_var = "education",   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "wide",   style = "report" ) #>   Variable Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking                                                       #> 2       No               179              69.6               415 #> 3      Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %      p Cramer's V #> 1                                                         < .001        .14 #> 2              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8                   #> 3              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2                   #>   CI lower CI upper #> 1      .08      .19 #> 2                   #> 3

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: Pass survey weights weights argument. Use rescale = TRUE total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   weights = "weight",   rescale = TRUE,   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: default, rows missing values dropped (drop_na = TRUE). Set drop_na = FALSE display “(Missing)” category:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "income_group",   group_var = "education",   drop_na = FALSE,   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: Use levels_keep display specific modalities control order:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   levels_keep = c("Yes"),   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: Control number digits percentages, p-values, association measure:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = "smoking",   group_var = "education",   percent_digits = 2,   p_digits = 4,   v_digits = 3,   output = "gt" )

title: APA tables with table_apa()
text: Excel export, provide file path: Word, use output = "word": can also copy directly clipboard pasting spreadsheet:
code: table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity", "dentist_12m"),   group_var = "education",   output = "excel",   excel_path = "my_table.xlsx" ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity", "dentist_12m"),   group_var = "education",   output = "word",   word_path = "my_table.docx" ) table_apa(   sochealth,   row_vars = c("smoking", "physical_activity"),   group_var = "education",   output = "clipboard" )

title: Descriptive Statistics and Data Management Tools
text: spicy R package first phase data analysis: inspecting variables, checking distributions, exploring associations, producing publication-ready tables. helps : Inspect variables varlist() — names, labels, values, classes, missings. Similar SPSS “Variable View” Stata “Variables Manager”. Explore distributions freq() associations cross_tab(). Measure associations cramer_v(), phi(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), somers_d(), — confidence intervals p-values. Build APA tables table_apa() — export gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard. Compute row-wise summaries mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n(). Generate codebooks code_book() — interactive HTML search, sort, export. Extract labels column headers label_from_names() — useful LimeSurvey CSV exports. Works labelled, factor, ordered, Date, POSIXct, common variable types. See vignette("spicy") full tour.

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: x data.frame spicy_table object containing table format. Typically, includes columns Category, Values, Freq., Percent, etc. padding Character string controlling horizontal spacing columns: "compact" — minimal spacing "normal" — moderate spacing (default) "wide" — extra spacing (large displays wide content) first_column_line Logical. TRUE (default), vertical separator drawn first column (useful separating categories data). row_total_line, column_total_line Logical. Control horizontal rules total rows columns. default TRUE. bottom_line Logical. FALSE (default), closing line drawn. TRUE, draws closing line bottom table. lines_color Character. Color used table separators. Defaults "darkgrey". color applied ANSI color support available (see crayon::has_color()). align_left_cols Integer vector column indices left-align. Defaults c(1, 2) frequency tables (Category + Values). ... Additional arguments (currently ignored).

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table

what: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: count_n() counts, row data frame matrix, many times one values appear across selected columns. supports type-safe comparison, case-insensitive string matching, detection special values NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
code: count_n(   data = NULL,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   count = NULL,   special = NULL,   allow_coercion = TRUE,   ignore_case = FALSE,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: data data frame matrix. Optional inside mutate(). select Columns include. Defaults tidyselect::everything(). Uses tidyselect helpers like tidyselect::starts_with(), etc. regex = TRUE, select treated regex string. exclude Character vector column names exclude selection. Defaults NULL (exclusion). count Value(s) count. Defaults NULL. Ignored special used. Multiple values allowed (e.g., count = c(1, 2, 3) count = c("yes", "")). R automatically coerces values count common type (e.g., c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2")), values expected final type. allow_coercion = FALSE, matching type-safe using identical(), type count must match values data. special Character vector special values count: "NA", "NaN", "Inf", "-Inf", "". Defaults NULL. "NA" uses .na(), therefore includes NA NaN values. "NaN" uses .nan() match actual NaN values. allow_coercion Logical. TRUE (default), values compared coercion. FALSE, uses strict matching via identical(). ignore_case Logical. FALSE (default), comparisons case-sensitive. TRUE, performs case-insensitive string comparisons. regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, interprets select regular expression pattern. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints processing messages.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: numeric vector row-wise counts (unnamed).

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: function particularly useful summarizing data quality patterns row-wise structures, designed work fluently inside dplyr::mutate() pipelines. Internally, count_n() wraps stable dependency-free base function base_count_n(), allowing high flexibility testability.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: function inspired datawizard::row_count(), provides additional flexibility: Element-wise type-safe matching using identical() allow_coercion = FALSE. ensures value type match exactly, enabling precise comparisons mixed-type columns. Support multiple values count, allowing queries like count = c(2, 3) count = c("yes", "") count several values per row. Detection special values NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf special argument — feature available row_count(). Tidyverse-native behavior: can used inside mutate() without explicitly passing data argument.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: R automatically coerces mixed-type vectors passed count common type. example, count = c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2"), R converts numeric character values unified type. means mixed-type checks possible runtime count passed function. ensure accurate type-sensitive matching, users avoid mixing types count explicitly.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: strict matching enabled, value count must match type target column exactly. factor columns, means count must also factor. Supplying count = "b" (character string) match factor value, even label appears identical. common intuitive approach use count = factor("b"), works many cases. However, identical() — used internally strict comparisons — also checks internal structure factor, including order content levels. result, comparisons may still fail levels differ, even label . ensure perfect match (label levels), can reuse value taken directly data (e.g., df$x[2]). guarantees class factor levels align. However, approach works reliably selected columns factor structure.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: ignore_case = TRUE, values involved comparison converted lowercase using tolower() matching. behavior applies character factor columns. Factors first converted character internally. Importantly, case-insensitive mode takes precedence strict type comparison: values longer compared using identical(), rather using lowercase string equality. enables flexible matching — example, "b" "B" match even allow_coercion = FALSE.

what: Example: strict vs. case-insensitive matching with factors
title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: Like datawizard::row_count(), function also supports regex-based column selection, case-insensitive string comparison, column exclusion.
code: df <- tibble::tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) )  # Strict match fails with character input count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Match works only where factor levels match exactly count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Case-insensitive match succeeds for both "b" and "B" count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tibble) library(haven)  # Basic usage df <- tibble(   x = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   y = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   z = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2") ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>       x     y z     #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr> #> 1     1     2 2     #> 2     2     2 2     #> 3     2    NA 2     #> 4     3     3 3     #> 5    NA     2 2     count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, count = "2", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 df |> mutate(num_twos = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>       x     y z     num_twos #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1     1     2 2            2 #> 2     2     2 2            3 #> 3     2    NA 2            2 #> 4     3     3 3            0 #> 5    NA     2 2            2  # Mixed types and special values df <- tibble(   num   = c(1, 2, NA, -Inf, NaN),   char  = c("a", "B", "b", "a", NA),   fact  = factor(c("a", "b", "b", "a", "c")),   date  = as.Date(c("2023-01-01", "2023-01-01", NA, "2023-01-02", "2023-01-01")),   lab   = labelled(c(1, 2, 1, 2, NA), labels = c(No = 1, Yes = 2)),   logic = c(TRUE, FALSE, NA, TRUE, FALSE) ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>     num char  fact  date       lab       logic #>   <dbl> <chr> <fct> <date>     <dbl+lbl> <lgl> #> 1     1 a     a     2023-01-01  1 [No]   TRUE  #> 2     2 B     b     2023-01-01  2 [Yes]  FALSE #> 3    NA b     b     NA          1 [No]   NA    #> 4  -Inf a     a     2023-01-02  2 [Yes]  TRUE  #> 5   NaN NA    c     2023-01-01 NA        FALSE count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 0 2 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0 0 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 2 0 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 0 2 2 0 0 count_n(df, count = "a", select = fact) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = as.Date("2023-01-01"), select = date) #> [1] 1 1 0 0 1 count_n(df, count = TRUE, select = logic) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = 2, select = lab) #> [1] 0 1 0 1 0 df <- df |> mutate(lab_chr = as_factor(lab)) count_n(df, count = "Yes", select = lab_chr, allow_coercion = TRUE) #> [1] 0 1 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = "Yes", select = lab_chr, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0 0 0  # Count special values count_n(df, special = "NA") #> [1] 0 0 3 0 4 count_n(df, special = "NaN") #> [1] 0 0 0 0 1 count_n(df, special = "-Inf") #> [1] 0 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, special = c("NA", "NaN")) #> [1] 0 0 3 0 4 count_n(df, special = "all") #> [1] 0 0 3 1 4  # Column selection strategies df <- tibble(   score_math    = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   score_science = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   score_lang    = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2"),   name          = c("Jean", "Marie", "Ali", "Zoe", "Nina") ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean  #> 2          2             2 2          Marie #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali   #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe   #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina  count_n(df, select = c(score_math, score_science), count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), exclude = "score_lang", count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = everything(), exclude = "name", count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, select = "^score_", regex = TRUE, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, select = "lang", regex = TRUE, count = "2") #> [1] 1 1 1 0 1 df |> mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean       2 #> 2          2             2 2          Marie      3 #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali        2 #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe        0 #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina       2 df |>   select(score_math, score_science) |>   mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   score_math score_science nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2      1 #> 2          2             2      2 #> 3          2            NA      1 #> 4          3             3      0 #> 5         NA             2      1 df$nb_two <- count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), count = 2) df[1:3, ] |> count_n(select = starts_with("score_"), count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2  # Strict type-safe matching with factor columns df <- tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) ) df #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   x     y     #>   <fct> <fct> #> 1 a     b     #> 2 b     B     #> 3 c     a      # Coercion: character "b" matches both x and y count_n(df, count = "b") #> [1] 1 1 0  # Strict match: fails because "b" is character, not factor (returns only 0s) count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Strict match with factor value: works only where levels match count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Using a value from the data: guarantees type and levels match for column x count_n(df, count = df$x[2], allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Case-insensitive match (factors are converted to character internally) count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0 count_n(df, count = "B", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: cramer_v() computes Cramer's V two-way contingency table, measuring strength association two categorical variables.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: cramer_v(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: detail = FALSE: single numeric value (estimate). detail = TRUE conf_level non-NULL: c(estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value). detail = TRUE conf_level = NULL: c(estimate, p_value). p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Cramer's V computed \(V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (n \cdot (k - 1))}\), \(\chi^2\) Pearson chi-squared statistic, \(n\) total count, \(k = \min(r, c)\). confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Agresti, . (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley. Liebetrau, . M. (1983). Measures Association. Sage. Signorell, . et al. (2024). DescTools: Tools Descriptive Statistics. R package.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) cramer_v(tab) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 1.356677e-01 7.909264e-02 1.913716e-01 2.012877e-05  cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #>     estimate      p_value  #> 1.356677e-01 2.012877e-05

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: Computes two-way cross-tabulation optional weights, grouping (including combinations multiple variables), percentage displays, inferential statistics. cross_tab() produces weighted unweighted contingency tables row column percentages, optional grouping via , associated Chi-squared tests association measure diagnostic information. x y variables required. one-way frequency tables, use freq() instead.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
code: cross_tab(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   by = NULL,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   percent = c("none", "column", "row"),   include_stats = TRUE,   assoc_measure = "auto",   assoc_ci = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   digits = NULL,   styled = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE )  # S3 method for class 'spicy_cross_table_list' print(x, ...)

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data data frame. Alternatively, vector using vector-based interface. x Row variable (unquoted). y Column variable (unquoted). Mandatory; one-way tables, use freq(). Optional grouping variable expression. Can single variable combination multiple variables (e.g. interaction(vs, )). weights Optional numeric weights. rescale Logical. FALSE (default), weights used -. TRUE, rescales weights total weighted N matches raw N. percent One "none" (default), "row", "column". Unique abbreviations accepted (e.g. "n", "r", "c"). include_stats Logical. TRUE (default), computes Chi-squared association measure (see assoc_measure). assoc_measure Character. association measure report. "auto" (default) selects Kendall's Tau-b variables ordered factors Cramer's V otherwise. choices: "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d", "lambda", "none". assoc_ci Logical. TRUE, includes 95 percent confidence interval association measure note. Defaults FALSE. correct Logical. FALSE (default), continuity correction applied. TRUE, applies Yates correction (2x2 tables). simulate_p Logical. FALSE (default), uses asymptotic p-values. TRUE, uses Monte Carlo simulation. simulate_B Integer. Number replicates Monte Carlo simulation. Defaults 2000. digits Number decimals. Defaults 1 percentages, 0 counts. styled Logical. TRUE (default), returns spicy_cross_table object (formatted printing). FALSE, returns plain data.frame. show_n Logical. TRUE (default), adds marginal N totals percent != "none". ... Additional arguments passed individual print methods.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data.frame, list data.frames, spicy_cross_table object. used, returns spicy_cross_table_list.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: function recognizes following global options modify default behavior: options(spicy.percent = "column") Sets default percentage mode calls cross_tab(). Valid values "none", "row", "column". Equivalent setting percent = "column" (another choice) call. options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE) Enables Monte Carlo simulation Chi-squared tests default. Equivalent setting simulate_p = TRUE every call. options(spicy.rescale = TRUE) Automatically rescales weights total weighted N equals raw N. Equivalent setting rescale = TRUE call. options convenient users wish enforce consistent behavior across multiple calls cross_tab() spicy table functions. can disabled reset setting NULL: options(spicy.percent = NULL, spicy.simulate_p = NULL, spicy.rescale = NULL). Example:
code: options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE, spicy.rescale = TRUE) cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight)

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: Creates frequency table vector variable data frame, options weighting, sorting, handling labelled data, defining custom missing values, displaying cumulative percentages. styled = TRUE, function prints spicy-formatted ASCII table using print.spicy_freq_table() spicy_print_table(); otherwise, returns data.frame containing frequencies proportions.

title: Frequency Table — freq
code: freq(   data,   x = NULL,   weights = NULL,   digits = 1,   valid = TRUE,   cum = FALSE,   sort = "",   na_val = NULL,   labelled_levels = c("prefixed", "labels", "values", "p", "l", "v"),   rescale = TRUE,   styled = TRUE,   ... )

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: data data.frame, vector, factor. data frame provided, specify target variable x. x variable data (unquoted). weights Optional numeric vector weights (length x). variable may referenced bare name belongs data. digits Number decimal digits display percentages (default: 1). valid Logical. TRUE (default), display valid percentages (excluding missing values). cum Logical. FALSE (default), cumulative percentages omitted. TRUE, adds cumulative percentages. sort Sorting method values: "" - sorting (default) "+" - increasing frequency "-" - decreasing frequency "name+" - alphabetical -Z "name-" - alphabetical Z-na_val Vector numeric character values treated missing (NA). labelled variables (haven labelled), argument must refer underlying coded values, visible labels. Example:   labelled_levels labelled variables, defines labels values displayed: "prefixed" "p" - show labels [value] label (default) "labels" "l" - show labels "values" "v" - show numeric codes rescale Logical. TRUE (default), rescale weights total equals unweighted sample size. styled Logical. TRUE (default), print formatted spicy table. FALSE, return plain data.frame frequency values. ... Additional arguments passed print.spicy_freq_table().
code: x <- labelled(c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3), c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3)) freq(x, na_val = 1) # Treat all "Low" as missing

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: data.frame columns: value - unique values factor levels n - frequency count (weighted applicable) prop - proportion total valid_prop - proportion valid responses (valid = TRUE) cum_prop, cum_valid_prop - cumulative percentages (cum = TRUE) styled = TRUE, prints formatted table console returns invisibly.

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: function designed mimic common frequency procedures statistical software SPSS Stata, integrating flexibility R's data structures. automatically detects type input (vector, factor, labelled) applies appropriate transformations, including: Handling labelled variables via labelled haven Optional recoding specific values missing (na_val) Optional weighting rescaling mechanism Support cumulative percentages (cum = TRUE) Multiple display modes labels via labelled_levels weighting applied (weights), frequencies percentages computed proportionally weights. argument rescale = TRUE normalizes weights sum equals unweighted sample size.

title: Frequency Table — freq
code: # Frequency table with labelled ordered factor freq(sochealth, education) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category │ Values           Freq.  Percent  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Lower secondary    261     21.8  #>           │ Upper secondary    539     44.9  #>           │ Tertiary           400     33.3  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │                   1200    100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth freq(sochealth, self_rated_health, sort = "-") #> Frequency table: self_rated_health #>  #>  Category │ Values     Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Good         558     46.5           47.3  #>           │ Very good    295     24.6           25.0  #>           │ Fair         266     22.2           22.5  #>           │ Poor          61      5.1            5.2  #>  Missing  │ NA            20      1.7                 #> ──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             1200    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Label: Self-rated health #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth  library(labelled)  # Simple numeric vector x <- c(1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, NA) freq(x) #> Frequency table: x #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ 1           1     14.3           16.7  #>           │ 2           2     28.6           33.3  #>           │ 3           3     42.9           50.0  #>  Missing  │ NA          1     14.3                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             7    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Class: numeric #> Data: x  # Labelled variable (haven-style) x_lbl <- labelled(   c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, NA),   labels = c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3) ) var_label(x_lbl) <- "Satisfaction level"  # Treat value 1 ("Low") as missing freq(x_lbl, na_val = 1) #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category │ Values      Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ [2] Medium      3     33.3           60.0  #>           │ [3] High        2     22.2           40.0  #>  Missing  │ NA              4     44.4                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │                 9    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # Display only labels, add cumulative % freq(x_lbl, labelled_levels = "labels", cum = TRUE) #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  Cum. Percent  Cum. Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Low         3     33.3           37.5          33.3                37.5  #>           │ Medium      3     33.3           37.5          66.7                75.0  #>           │ High        2     22.2           25.0          88.9               100.0  #>  Missing  │ NA          1     11.1                        100.0                      #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             9    100.0          100.0         100.0               100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # Display values only, sorted descending freq(x_lbl, labelled_levels = "values", sort = "-") #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ 1           3     33.3           37.5  #>           │ 2           3     33.3           37.5  #>           │ 3           2     22.2           25.0  #>  Missing  │ NA          1     11.1                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             9    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # With weighting df <- data.frame(   sexe = factor(c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", NA, "Female")),   poids = c(12, 8, 10, 15, 7, 9) )  # Weighted frequencies (normalized) freq(df, sexe, weights = poids, rescale = TRUE) #> Frequency table: sexe #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Female   2.66     44.3           50.0  #>           │ Male     2.66     44.3           50.0  #>  Missing  │ NA       0.69     11.5                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             6    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: poids (rescaled)  # Weighted frequencies (without rescaling) freq(df, sexe, weights = poids, rescale = FALSE) #> Frequency table: sexe #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Female     27     44.3           50.0  #>           │ Male       27     44.3           50.0  #>  Missing  │ NA          7     11.5                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │            61    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: poids  # Base R style, with weights and cumulative percentages freq(df$sexe, weights = df$poids, cum = TRUE) #> Frequency table: sexe #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq. Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Female   2.66     44.3           50.0          44.3                50.0  #>           │ Male     2.66     44.3           50.0          88.5               100.0  #>  Missing  │ NA       0.69     11.5                        100.0                      #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             6    100.0          100.0         100.0               100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: poids (rescaled)  # Piped version (tidy syntax) and sort alphabetically descending ("name-") df |> freq(sexe, sort = "name-") #> Frequency table: sexe #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  Valid Percent  #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Male        2     33.3           40.0  #>           │ Female      3     50.0           60.0  #>  Missing  │ NA          1     16.7                 #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total    │             6    100.0          100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df  # Non-styled return (for programmatic use) f <- freq(df, sexe, styled = FALSE) head(f) #> Frequency table: sexe #>  #>  Category │ Values  Freq. title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: gamma_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal Gamma statistic two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: gamma_gk(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: gamma = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: Gamma computed \(\gamma = (C - D) / (C + D)\), \(C\) \(D\) numbers concordant discordant pairs. ignores tied pairs, making appropriate ordinal variables many ties. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) gamma_gk(tab) #> [1] 0.3104791 gamma_gk(tab, detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 3.104791e-01 2.376258e-01 3.833324e-01 6.667393e-17

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(   x,   direction = c("row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row) "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: Unlike lambda_gk(), Goodman-Kruskal's Tau uses cell frequencies rather modal categories, making sensitive association patterns lambda may zero. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) goodman_kruskal_tau(tab) #> [1] 0.01840572 goodman_kruskal_tau(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #> 0.007609460 0.001118492 0.014100428 0.021579123

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: kendall_tau_b() computes Kendall's Tau-b two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: kendall_tau_b(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: Kendall's Tau-b computed \(\tau_b = (C - D) / \sqrt{(n_0 - n_1)(n_0 - n_2)}\), \(n_0 = n(n-1)/2\), \(n_1\) number pairs tied row variable, \(n_2\) number tied column variable. Tau-b corrects ties appropriate square tables. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_b(tab) #> [1] 0.2045524

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: kendall_tau_c(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: Stuart's Tau-c computed \(\tau_c = 2m(C - D) / (n^2(m - 1))\), \(m = \min(r, c)\). appropriate rectangular tables restricted range \([-1, 1]\) square tables. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_c(tab) #> [1] 0.1996409

what: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: Splits column name first occurrence sep, renames column part sep (name), assigns part sep labelled::var_label(). works even label contains separator.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: label_from_names(df, sep = ". ")

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: df data.frame tibble column names form "name<sep>label" (e.g. "name. label"). (default LimeSurvey). sep Character string used separator name label. Default ". " (LimeSurvey's default), literal string can used.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: base tibble column names equal names (sep) var_label attributes equal labels (sep).

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: function especially useful LimeSurvey CSV exports using Export results → Export format: CSV → Headings: Question code & question text, column names look like "code. question text". case default separator ". ".

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: # Example with LimeSurvey-style column names df <- data.frame(   "age. Age of respondent" = c(25, 30),   "score. Total score. Manually computed." = c(12, 14),   check.names = FALSE )  # sep = ". " by default (LimeSurvey) out <- label_from_names(df) labelled::var_label(out) #> $age #> [1] "Age of respondent" #>  #> $score #> [1] "Total score. Manually computed." #>   # Example with a custom separator ("|") df2 <- data.frame(   "id|Identifier" = 1:3,   "score|Total score" = c(10, 20, 30),   check.names = FALSE ) out2 <- label_from_names(df2, sep = "|") labelled::var_label(out2) #> $id #> [1] "Identifier" #>  #> $score #> [1] "Total score" #>

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: lambda_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda, proportional reduction error (PRE) measure nominal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: lambda_gk(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: Lambda measures much prediction error reduced independent variable used predict dependent variable. ranges 0 (reduction) 1 (perfect prediction). Lambda can equal zero even variables associated modal category dominates every column (row). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) lambda_gk(tab) #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> estimate ci_lower ci_upper  p_value  #>        0        0        0       NA

what: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: mean_n() computes row means data.frame matrix, handling missing values (NAs) automatically. Row-wise means calculated across selected numeric columns, optional condition minimum number (proportion) valid (non-missing) values required row included. Non-numeric columns excluded automatically reported.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
code: mean_n(   data = NULL,   select = dplyr::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   min_valid = NULL,   digits = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: data data.frame matrix. select Columns include. regex = FALSE, use tidyselect syntax (default: dplyr::everything()). regex = TRUE, provide regular expression pattern (character string). exclude Columns exclude (default: NULL). min_valid Minimum number valid (non-NA) values required per row. proportion, applied number selected columns. Defaults NULL (values must valid). digits Optional number decimal places round result. Defaults NULL (rounding). regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, select argument treated regular expression. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints message non-numeric columns excluded.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: numeric vector row-wise means.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
code: library(dplyr)  # Create a simple numeric data frame df <- tibble(   var1 = c(10, NA, 30, 40, 50),   var2 = c(5, NA, 15, NA, 25),   var3 = c(NA, 30, 20, 50, 10) )  # Compute row-wise mean (all values must be valid by default) mean_n(df) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Round the result to 1 decimal mean_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1]   NA   NA 21.7   NA 28.3  # Select specific columns mean_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mean_n() #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Exclude a column mean_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select columns ending with "1" mean_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       22.5 #> 4    40    NA    50       40   #> 5    50    25    10       37.5  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA       NA   #> 3    30    15       22.5 #> 4    40    NA       40   #> 5    50    25       37.5  # Show verbose processing info df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> mean_n(): Row means computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored mean_n(df_mixed) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use within mutate() on mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # Use everything() but exclude non-numeric columns manually mean_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Select columns using regex mean_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333 mean_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Apply to a subset of rows (first 3) df_mixed[1:3, ] |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$mean_score <- mean_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # With a numeric matrix mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, NA, 7, 8, 9), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) mat #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    2   NA #> [2,]    4    5   NA #> [3,]    7    8    9 mat |> mean_n(min_valid = 2) #> [1] 1.5 4.5 8.0

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: phi coefficient \(\phi = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\). equivalent Cramer's V 2x2 tables equals Pearson correlation two binary variables. confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Phi coefficient — phi

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: somers_d() computes Somers' D two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: somers_d(   x,   direction = c("row", "column", "symmetric"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column), "symmetric" (average directions). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: Somers' D asymmetric ordinal measure defined \(d = (C - D) / (C + D + T)\), \(T\) number pairs tied independent variable. symmetric version harmonic mean two asymmetric values. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) somers_d(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0.2015369 somers_d(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #>     estimate     ci_lower     ci_upper      p_value  #> 2.076131e-01 1.573452e-01 2.578810e-01 5.730469e-16

what: spicy: Descriptive Statistics and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
title: spicy: Descriptive Statistics and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
text: Provides tools early data exploration, variable inspection, quick tabulation workflows 'R'. Summarizes variable metadata, labels, classes, missing values, representative values, support readable frequency tables, cross-tabulations, association measures contingency tables (Cramer's V, Phi, Goodman-Kruskal Gamma, Kendall's Tau-b, Somers' D, others), APA-style reporting tables. Includes helpers interactive codebooks, variable label extraction, clipboard export, row-wise descriptive summaries. Designed make initial data analysis faster, clearer, easier work practice.

Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row sum_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1] 15 NA 65 90 85  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row sum_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1] 15 NA 65 90 85  # Round the results to 1 decimal sum_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Select specific columns sum_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   sum_n() #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Exclude a column sum_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Select columns ending with "1" sum_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> sum_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        65 #> 4    40    NA    50        90 #> 5    50    25    10        85  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        45 #> 4    40    NA    50        40 #> 5    50    25    10        75  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5        15 #> 2    NA    NA        NA #> 3    30    15        45 #> 4    40    NA        40 #> 5    50    25        75  # Show verbose message df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> sum_n(): Row sums computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        65 #> 4    40    NA    50        90 #> 5    50    25    10        85  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored sum_n(df_mixed) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Use inside mutate with mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A            NA #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A            NA #> 3    30    15    20 c     B            65 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B            NA #> 5    50    25    10 e     A            85  # Use everything(), but exclude known non-numeric sum_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Select columns using regex sum_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85 sum_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Apply to a subset of rows df_mixed[1:3, ] |> sum_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1] NA NA 65  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$sum_score <- sum_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A            NA #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A            NA #> 3    30    15    20 c     B            65 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B            NA #> 5    50    25    10 e     A            85  # title: Build APA-Style Cross-Tabulation Tables — table_apa
text: table_apa() builds publication-ready table crossing one grouping variable (group_var) one many row variables (row_vars), using spicy::cross_tab() internally.

title: Build APA-Style Cross-Tabulation Tables — table_apa
code: table_apa(   data,   row_vars,   group_var,   labels = NULL,   levels_keep = NULL,   include_total = TRUE,   drop_na = TRUE,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   percent_digits = 1,   p_digits = 3,   v_digits = 2,   assoc_measure = "auto",   assoc_ci = FALSE,   decimal_mark = ".",   output = c("wide", "long", "tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard",     "word"),   style = c("auto", "raw", "report"),   indent_text = "  ",   indent_text_excel_clipboard = "      ",   add_multilevel_header = TRUE,   blank_na_wide = FALSE,   excel_path = NULL,   excel_sheet = "APA",   clipboard_delim = "\t",   word_path = NULL )

title: Build APA-Style Cross-Tabulation Tables — table_apa
text: data data frame. row_vars Character vector variable names place rows. group_var Single character variable name used columns/groups. labels Optional character labels row_vars (length). levels_keep Optional character vector levels keep/order row modalities. NULL, observed levels kept. include_total Logical. TRUE (default), includes Total group available. drop_na Logical. TRUE (default), removes rows NA row/group variable cross-tabulation. FALSE, missing values displayed dedicated "(Missing)" level. weights Optional weights. Either NULL (default), numeric vector length nrow(data), single column name data. rescale Logical. FALSE (default), weights used -. TRUE, rescales weights total weighted N matches raw N. Passed spicy::cross_tab(). correct Logical. FALSE (default), continuity correction applied. TRUE, applies Yates correction 2x2 chi-squared contexts. Passed spicy::cross_tab(). simulate_p Logical. FALSE (default), uses asymptotic p-values. TRUE, uses Monte Carlo simulation. Passed spicy::cross_tab(). simulate_B Integer. Number Monte Carlo replicates simulate_p = TRUE. Defaults 2000. percent_digits Number digits percentages report outputs. Defaults 1. p_digits Number digits p-values (except < .001). Defaults 3. v_digits Number digits association measure. Defaults 2. assoc_measure Passed cross_tab(). association measure report ("auto", "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d", "lambda", "none"). Defaults "auto". assoc_ci Passed cross_tab(). TRUE, includes confidence interval. data/export formats (wide, long, excel, clipboard), two extra columns CI lower CI upper added. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable, word), CI shown inline .14 [.08, .19] association measure column. Defaults FALSE. decimal_mark Decimal separator ("." ","). Defaults ".". output Output format: "wide" (default), "long", "tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard", "word". style "auto" (default) select output type, "raw" machine-friendly outputs, "report" formatted outputs. indent_text Prefix used modality labels report table building. Defaults "  " (two spaces). indent_text_excel_clipboard Stronger indentation used Excel clipboard exports. Defaults six non-breaking spaces. add_multilevel_header Logical. TRUE (default), merges top headers Excel export. blank_na_wide Logical. FALSE (default), NA values kept -wide raw output. TRUE, replaces empty strings. excel_path Path output = "excel". Defaults NULL. excel_sheet Sheet name Excel export. Defaults "APA". clipboard_delim Delimiter clipboard text export. Defaults "\t". word_path Path output = "word" optional save path output = "flextable". Defaults NULL.

title: Build APA-Style Cross-Tabulation Tables — table_apa
text: Depends output style: "long" + "raw": long numeric data frame. "wide" + "raw": wide numeric data frame. "long" + "report": long formatted character data frame. "wide" + "report": wide formatted character data frame. "tinytable": tinytable object. "gt": gt_tbl object. "flextable": flextable object. "excel" / "clipboard" / "word": invisibly returns written object/path.

title: Build APA-Style Cross-Tabulation Tables — table_apa
text: supports raw data outputs (wide, long) report-oriented outputs (tinytable, flextable, excel, clipboard, word) multi-level headers, p-values, association measure. Optional output engines require suggested packages: tinytable output = "tinytable" gt output = "gt" flextable + officer output = "flextable"/"word" openxlsx output = "excel" clipr output = "clipboard"

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty_coef() computes Uncertainty Coefficient (Theil's U) two-way contingency table, based information entropy.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: uncertainty_coef(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty coefficient measures association using Shannon entropy. direction = "row": \(U = (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / H_X\), \(H_X\), \(H_Y\) marginal entropies \(H_{XY}\) joint entropy. symmetric version \(U = 2 (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / (H_X + H_Y)\). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) uncertainty_coef(tab) #> [1] 0.01148762 uncertainty_coef(tab, direction = "row", detail = TRUE) #>    estimate    ci_lower    ci_upper     p_value  #> 0.017512300 0.002649834 0.032374766 0.020921026

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: yule_q() computes Yule's Q coefficient association 2x2 contingency table.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
code: yule_q(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: 2x2 table cells \(, b, c, d\), Yule's Q \(Q = (ad - bc) / (ad + bc)\). equivalent Goodman-Kruskal Gamma 2x2 tables. asymptotic standard error \(SE = 0.5 (1 - Q^2) \sqrt{1/+ 1/b + 1/c + 1/d}\). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.
